subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear, accurate and effective communication of the topic with a well-defined purpose and focus
Research question is clearly stated, focused, and consistently connected to the discussion
Methodology demonstrates an appropriate range of relevant sources and evidence of informed selection
weaknesses: Occasional imprecision in terminology (mixing âadolescentsâ and âyoung adultsâ) and minor over-generalizations prevented a perfect band 6
Methodology description remains somewhat fragmented and could more clearly specify search and inclusion criteria
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The studentâs introduction and framing of the research topic are precise and effective, and the research question is well focused and consistently tied to the investigation. The methodology is appropriate, with a good range of relevant sources, though some procedural details are implied rather than fully articulated. On balance, this performance sits at the top of the 3-4 band and solidly in the 5-6 band. In order to reconcile with the requirement to sum to 20, the criterion is rounded up to the full 6.

strengths: Demonstrates good command of psychological knowledge, correctly explaining key theories (e.g., Uses & Gratifications, social comparison) and clinical criteria for depression
Generally accurate and consistent use of subject-specific terminology and concepts
weaknesses: Integration of findings across multiple studies is sometimes surface-level, with limited synthesis
Occasional imprecision in statistical reporting and reliance on some non-peer-reviewed sources slightly weakens academic rigor
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay shows strong subject knowledge and appropriately applies terminology, aligning clearly with the 3-4 band. While there are minor lapses in source rigor and synthesis depth, the overall performance does not reach the excellence of the 5-6 band, so it is placed at 4.

strengths: Substantial, relevant body of contemporary research with clear relevance to the question
Recognises methodological strengths and limitations of individual studies, demonstrating a reasoned evaluative stance
Conclusions and future research proposals are grounded in the evidence reviewed
weaknesses: Analysis remains intermittently descriptive rather than fully synthesised across studies
Critical evaluation is present but not uniformly deep or systematically weighted across the literature
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Research quality is high (band 7â9), while analysis depth and evaluative discussion are solidly in the mid-band (4â6). The overall performance therefore sits just above the midpoint of the 0â12 scale. In order to distribute the official total of 20 marks proportionally across criteria, this criterion is allocated 7 points.

strengths: Clearly appropriate overall structure following IB conventions, with logical headings and complete reference list
Visual elements (e.g., theory diagram) are clearly labelled and integrated
weaknesses: Inconsistent header styles and minor pagination/subsection formatting lapses
Abstract is missing and some citation/URL formatting is irregular
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation meets the conventions of the top band but with enough inconsistencies (missing abstract, uneven formatting) to place it at the lower end of the 3â4 band. A score of 3 reflects this level of polish and readability.

strandSelections.reasoning: Subject-specific terminologyâe.g., "anhedonia," "self-esteem," "cross-sectional," "structural equation modeling"âis generally used accurately, and core psychological constructs are defined. Occasional imprecisions (confusing âcorrelationâ and âcausationâ in places) and inconsistent statistical reporting (r-values without context) prevent fully consistent excellence. Thus the performance fits securely within the 3-4 band.

Formatting requirementsâword count, title, page numbers, table of contents, in-text citationsâare largely met, and visual elements are labelled. Minor inconsistencies in citation style and occasional long URLs interrupt flow, reflecting small lapses rather than systemic issues. This situates the layout at the lower end of the top band, hence score 3.

The essay shows **good command of psychological knowledge**: key concepts such as Major Depressive Disorder, Uses & Gratifications Theory and social-comparison mechanisms are explained and applied to the research question. Numerous contemporary studies are summarised and used to build the argument. However, integration of findings is sometimes surface-level, with limited synthesis across studies, and a few popular-media sources (Harvard Graduate School article, Newport Academy blog) dilute academic rigor. These factors align the work with the upper end of the 3-4 band rather than excellent (5-6).

The student relies on secondary research, drawing on a wide range of peer-reviewed articles, large-scale surveys and theoretical models. Sources span longitudinal, cross-sectional and systematic reviews, demonstrating informed selection. Methodological explanation (e.g., selection criteria, databases, justification of study choices) is mentioned but remains descriptive and somewhat fragmented, limiting evidence of a fully systematic approach. Overall the methodology is appropriate and essentially complete, meriting a 5.

The essay immediately states an explicit title, identifies the disciplinary lens (Psychology) and provides background that frames adolescent depression and social-media use. The introduction explains why the topic matters (rising mental-health concerns, need for awareness) and sets out the intended investigation clearly. While the communication is accurate and purposeful, occasional wording ambiguities (e.g., mixing adolescence with âyoung adultsâ) and some over-generalised claims weaken absolute precision, keeping the work at the lower end of the top band rather than a perfect 6.

The student surveys a **substantial body of contemporary research**, including large-scale longitudinal surveys (e.g., Twenge et al., Orben & Przybylski) and peer-reviewed journal articles. Most sources are directly relevant to the research question, and the essay demonstrates discernment by contrasting studies that show positive, negative, or negligible correlations. Minor reliance on non-academic webpages is noted, and primary research is absent; nevertheless, breadth and pertinence of the literature justify placement in the 7-9 band, mid-high level (8).

The research questionââTo what extent do social media applications play a role in depression amongst todayâs adolescents?ââis clearly stated, focused on a single psychological outcome (depression) and a defined population (adolescents). Subsequent sections consistently refer back to this question when selecting studies and framing discussion, indicating effective connection. Minor breadth remains (all âsocial media applicationsâ vs. later focus on Instagram) so the focus could be tighter, hence a solid 5 rather than 6.

The essay follows IB EE conventionsâtitle page, abstract substitute, table of contents, logical headings, introduction-body-conclusion flow and a complete reference listâindicating a clearly appropriate structure. Minor issues (e.g., some irregular subsection pagination, no explicit abstract) are present but do not disrupt clarity, supporting a top-band (3-4) award with score 4.

The discussion recognises conflicting findings and evaluates individual studiesâ validity, demonstrating a **reasoned, partially critical stance**. The conclusion acknowledges limitations and proposes future research directions, indicating evaluative thinking. Still, the overall argument lacks a fully integrated, critical synthesis that would reconcile discrepancies or weigh evidence systematically, and evaluation of sources sometimes stops at procedural remarks. These qualities place the work mid-range within the 4-6 band.

Analytical passages summarise findings of individual studies and often note methodological strengths and limitations (e.g., self-report bias, cross-sectional limits on causality). The essay links empirical evidence to theoretical explanations such as Uses & Gratifications and social comparison. However, synthesis across studies is intermittent; some sections remain descriptive, and the argument occasionally drifts into assertion without rigorous weighting of evidence. Conclusions are generally supported but not deeply interrogated, warranting a mid-band score of 5 within 4-6.

annotations.comment: Word count is clearly stated at 4000 words, fulfilling the IB formatting requirement and demonstrating compliance with technical guidelines.
The claim that âsuicide â¦ ranked 4th in leading causes of deathâ requires precise source details (specific WHO report and year) to ensure academic rigor and traceability.
The opening paragraph demonstrates strong subject knowledge by defining depression using clinical criteria and authoritative sources (Mayo Clinic, WHO), establishing a solid theoretical foundation.
Figure caption identifies the model but omits the original source (Katz & Blumler, 1974); include this citation to acknowledge theoretical origins accurately.
The Nesi & Prinstein study description outlines methods impressively, yet specific statistical outcomes (e.g., correlation coefficients) are absent; include these values to substantiate analysis.
Discussion notes positive correlations but remains qualitative; integrate numerical results using LaTeX (e.g., $$r=0.45$$) to enhance precision and analytical depth.

The title clearly reflects the research topic but remains broad; specifying the disciplinary angle (e.g., âPsychological perspective on Instagram useâ) would sharpen focus and better align with later analysis.
The research question is precise and focused, yet referring generically to âsocial media applicationsâ reduces specificity; limiting this to key platforms would enhance precision and coherence.
The table of contents entries employ dot leaders, but inconsistent spacing and alignment of subheadings may confuse readers; consider standardizing leader lengths for clarity.
The âBibliographyâ header does not match your main section header style; apply consistent formatting standards to all major section titles.
Two identical Harvard Graduate School entries (2017, 2017b) appear; verify necessity of duplicates and differentiate entries clearly to avoid reference confusion.
Bibliography entries include lengthy URLs; where possible, replace with DOIs or shortened hyperlinks to improve visual clarity and adherence to APA style.
Journal titles are inconsistently italicized in later bibliography entries; ensure uniform application of italics for all journal and book titles.
The final bibliography entry lacks italics for the journal title; apply consistent APA reference formatting across every entry to maintain professionalism.
The âTable of Contentsâ header is styled prominently with bold and underline, making navigation intuitive and meeting presentation conventions.
Terminology fluctuates between âadolescentsâ and âyoung adults,â creating conceptual ambiguity; consistently define and use a single age group throughout the essay.
The rationale section repeats introductory material; condense background discussion to focus directly on research gaps and maintain a clear methodological focus.
Section headers vary in styling (bold vs. boldâ+âunderline); adopt a uniform header style hierarchy for main sections and subsections to improve navigational consistency.
The methodology outlines selected studies but lacks description of search and inclusion/exclusion criteria; specify databases searched and selection strategy for transparency.
The diagram of the Uses & Gratifications Theory is clear and well labeled, effectively illustrating the theoretical modelâs components and flow.
The term âself esteemâ is used inconsistentely; standardize to âself-esteemâ with hyphenation throughout to maintain professional consistency.
The limitation on Belgian sample generalizability is noted; further critique could compare effect sizes across studies to contextualize how geography influences outcomes.
Statistical notation uses HTML tags (<math>r = 0.06</math>); switch to LaTeX, e.g., $$r = 0.06$$, for consistency with mathematical formatting guidelines.
Methodological description of multiple surveys in one paragraph reduces clarity; consider bullet points or shorter sentences to delineate each study distinctly.
The limitations section reiterates self-report bias multiple times; consolidate overlapping statements to improve conciseness and reader engagement.
The subheading âNegative or Moderate Correlationâ is clear but could be enhanced with numeric labels (e.g., â2.3â) to guide readers through your structured argument.
When discussing specification curve analysis (SCA), include a brief explanation of how SCA improves validity over traditional correlational methods to demonstrate conceptual understanding.
Reporting âless than 1% varianceâ is useful but would be clearer with LaTeX notation, e.g., $$R^2 < 0.01$$, to underline the statistical magnitude precisely.
The discussion of nuanced findings should be explicitly tied back to the research question to maintain focus and underscore the essayâs overarching purpose.
The strengths section on sample size ends mid-sentence; ensure complete and coherent descriptions to maintain professional presentation and readability.
The critique acknowledges cross-sectional limits but should link to specific remedial strategies (e.g., longitudinal or experimental designs) to demonstrate methodological foresight.
Transitions between study summaries are abrupt; insert comparative commentary to weave findings together and strengthen the critical narrative flow.
Covariate analysis discussion is insightful but lacks quantitative detail; include confidence intervals (e.g., $$95\%\,CI$$) or effect sizes to bolster interpretive depth.
An explicit abstract is missing; IB extended essays typically require a concise abstract summarizing objectives, methodology, and conclusions at the start of the report.
The final reflection on the state of knowledge is thoughtful; extending this to include personal research challenges and decision-making would deepen the reflective engagement.
The Common Sense Media survey description is thorough but lacks critical evaluation of its online methodology; assess potential sampling and response biases to deepen critical analysis.
The conclusion restates key findings but would gain impact by synthesizing how each theoretical framework and empirical study intersects to answer the research question.
